title: Slewing
subtitle: Weaving the sides of a basket quickly with multiple chasing rods
excerpt: Slewing is the fast multi-rod technique for building up the body of a willow basket: two or more rods are inserted behind adjacent stakes and chased around the basket together, each one weaving in front of the rod ahead. The result covers the body quickly and produces a distinctive ribbed texture. This technique covers the two-rod slew, the insertion sequence, and the joins that keep the work even.
sourceNotes: Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for basket-weaving techniques. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for willow basket construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A basketry technique in which two or more weaving rods are inserted behind adjacent stakes and chased around the basket together, each passing in front of the rod immediately ahead of it.
Weaving with a single rod alternately in front of one stake and behind the next. Randing builds a fine, even surface; slewing builds faster with a ribbed texture.
A structural border row woven with three or more rods in sequence. Wales appear at regular intervals within a slewed body to reinforce the structure.

content.text: Slewing
covers the sides of a basket roughly three times faster than
randing

because multiple rods build height simultaneously as they chase around the stakes. The trade-off is a coarser, ribbed surface texture; this is appropriate for market baskets, storage baskets, and any form where speed and strength matter more than a fine even weave.
Wales
at regular intervals interrupt the slewing to keep the stakes evenly spaced and the walls from distorting.
What it does

Two rods inserted behind adjacent stakes chase each other around the full circumference of the basket. Each rod weaves in front of the rod immediately ahead of it and behind the stake; then the rod behind it leaps past in the same motion. The result is a spiral of overlapping rods that builds the wall height quickly.
When to use it

On any large basket where body height is needed quickly: market baskets, log baskets, laundry baskets. Not for fine decorative work, fine-weave panels, or the first and last two rows at the border, which should always be randed or waled for neatness.
What you need
Soaked weaving rods, 3 to 4 mm diameter, two per slewing pass. A rapping iron to pack the slewing down after each round. A sloyd knife for trimming joins.
Safety
No specific hazard. Keep the rapping iron strike directed downward, away from the free hand.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Insert two rods behind adjacent stakes

After completing any border wale, insert rod A behind one stake and rod B behind the stake immediately to the right. Both rod butts project to the left of their insertion stakes, toward the inside of the basket.
Step 2: Chase the left rod forward

Take rod A (the left rod). Weave it in front of the next stake and behind the stake after that, advancing it past rod B. Rod B is now the left rod. Take rod B and weave it in the same motion, advancing past where rod A now rests. Continue: always take the left (rearmost) rod and advance it one full in-front-behind weave.
Step 3: Pack after each round

After each full revolution of the basket, use the rapping iron to pack the slewing down firmly against the wale below. Uneven packing produces a wavy wall; firm, even packing produces a straight wall.
Step 4: Join new rods

When a rod runs out, insert the new rod behind the same stake where the old one exits, with the butt of the new rod overlapping the last 5 cm of the old one. The old rod lies under the new rod's first two weave passes and is hidden in the work. Never join two rods at adjacent stakes; stagger joins by at least three stakes.
What it looks like when it is right

A regular diagonal rib pattern visible on the outside face of the basket. Each rod spirals up the wall at a consistent angle. The stakes are evenly spaced and the wall is vertical; any leaning stakes should be corrected by a wale before the slewing continues.
Common mistakes
Advancing the wrong rod: always take the rearmost (leftmost) rod. Taking the forward rod produces a tangled cross instead of a clean spiral.
Not packing after each round: the wall grows uneven and the stakes lean. Pack firmly after every complete revolution.
